faqs.a: No. The World Health Organization recognizes burnout in the ICD-11 as an occupational phenomenon, not a formal psychiatric diagnosis in the DSM. That framing points to the role and its demands, not just the person, as part of the problem.

faqs.q: What's the difference between burnout and depression?
faqs.a: Burnout is tied specifically to work and tends to ease when the work situation changes, while depression touches many areas of life regardless of the job. The two can overlap and look alike, so a clinician can help tell them apart.
Ordinary fatigue usually lifts with rest, but burnout is the kind of exhaustion that rest alone doesn't fix, because the underlying chronic stress keeps going.
